(born 13 May 1983) is an Ivorian professional [football coach](/wiki/Coach_(association_football) "Coach (association football)") and former [player](/wiki/Football_player "Football player") who played as a [midfielder](/wiki/Midfielder "Midfielder"). He is currently an assistant coach for the [Saudi Arabia national team](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia_national_football_team "Saudi Arabia national football team").

**Gnégnéri Yaya Touré** (born 13 May 1983) is an Ivorian professional [football coach](/wiki/Coach_(association_football) "Coach (association football)") and former [player](/wiki/Football_player "Football player") who played as a [midfielder](/wiki/Midfielder "Midfielder"). He is currently an assistant coach for the [Saudi Arabia national team](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia_national_football_team "Saudi Arabia national football team").

Touré aspired to be a [striker](/wiki/Striker_(association_football) "Striker (association football)") during his youth[[4]](#cite_note-4) and has played [centre-back](/wiki/Centre-back "Centre-back"), including for [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") in the [2009 UEFA Champions League final](/wiki/2009_UEFA_Champions_League_final "2009 UEFA Champions League final").[[5]](#cite_note-centreback-5) However, he spent the majority of his career as a [box-to-box midfielder](/wiki/Box-to-box_midfielder "Box-to-box midfielder") for club and country, where he has been regarded as one of the world's best players in his position.[[6]](#cite_note-6) One of the greatest African players of all time, Touré was voted [African Footballer of the Year](/wiki/African_Footballer_of_the_Year "African Footballer of the Year") for 2011, 2012, 2013 and 2014.[[7]](#cite_note-AFOTY-7)[[8]](#cite_note-The_Guardian-8)

Touré began his playing career at Ivorian club [ASEC Mimosas](/wiki/ASEC_Mimosas "ASEC Mimosas"), where he made his debut at age 18. His performances attracted attention from Europe. He had stints with [Beveren](/wiki/K.S.K._Beveren "K.S.K. Beveren"), [Metalurh Donetsk](/wiki/FC_Metalurh_Donetsk "FC Metalurh Donetsk"), [Olympiacos](/wiki/Olympiacos_F.C. "Olympiacos F.C.") and [Monaco](/wiki/AS_Monaco_FC "AS Monaco FC") before moving to Barcelona in 2007. He played over 100 matches for the club and was part of the historic Barcelona team that won six trophies in a calendar year, in 2009. In 2010, Touré moved to Premier League club [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C."), where he scored a number of key goals, most notably the only goals in the 2011 [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") semi-final and [final](/wiki/2011_FA_Cup_final "2011 FA Cup final"). He also helped City earn their first [league title](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Premier_League "2011–12 Premier League") in 44 years.

Touré earned 100 [caps](/wiki/Cap_(association_football) "Cap (association football)") for the Ivory Coast from 2004 to 2015, representing the nation at the [2006](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup"), [2010](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup "2010 FIFA World Cup") and [2014 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup") tournaments. He also represented them in six [Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/Africa_Cup_of_Nations "Africa Cup of Nations") in [2006](/wiki/2006_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2006 Africa Cup of Nations"), [2008](/wiki/2008_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2008 Africa Cup of Nations"), [2010](/wiki/2010_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2010 Africa Cup of Nations"), [2012](/wiki/2012_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2012 Africa Cup of Nations"), [2013](/wiki/2013_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2013 Africa Cup of Nations") and [2015](/wiki/2015_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2015 Africa Cup of Nations"), helping them finish runner-up in 2006 and 2012, while [captaining](/wiki/Captain_(association_football) "Captain (association football)") them to victory in 2015. He is the younger brother of fellow former footballer [Kolo Touré](/wiki/Kolo_Tour%C3%A9 "Kolo Touré") who was his teammate at Manchester City and the national team.

## Club career

### Early career

Touré was born in [Bouaké](/wiki/Bouak%C3%A9 "Bouaké").[[9]](#cite_note-9) He joined the [ASEC Mimosas](/wiki/ASEC_Mimosas "ASEC Mimosas") youth academy in 1996 on the recommendation of his long-time mentor Patrick van Reijendam. A prominent figure in the ASEC academy was [Jean-Marc Guillou](/wiki/Jean-Marc_Guillou "Jean-Marc Guillou"). In 2001, Guillou invested heavily in a Belgian club, [Beveren](/wiki/K.S.K._Beveren "K.S.K. Beveren"), with the aim of using the team to showcase Ivorian players in a European league.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Touré moved to Beveren in 2001, one of many ASEC players to do so in this period. By 2003, he was one of 14 Ivorians in the Beveren squad.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the summer of 2003, Touré had a trial with [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.").[[11]](#cite_note-11) He started a pre-season [friendly](/wiki/Friendly_(association_football) "Friendly (association football)") against [Barnet](/wiki/Barnet_F.C. "Barnet F.C.") on 19 July which finished a 0–0 draw. The [BBC Sport](/wiki/BBC_Sport "BBC Sport") website said that Touré "blotted his copy book by missing Arsenal's clearest chance of the game, heading a cross from [Quincy Owusu-Abeyie](/wiki/Quincy_Owusu-Abeyie "Quincy Owusu-Abeyie") wide."[[12]](#cite_note-12) Wenger was still keen to sign the then 20-year-old but Touré had difficulties in receiving a [work permit](/wiki/Work_permit "Work permit").[[13]](#cite_note-13) Ultimately, Touré grew impatient and opted to sign for Ukrainian club [Metalurh Donetsk](/wiki/FC_Metalurh_Donetsk "FC Metalurh Donetsk") in December 2003, where he spent one and a half years.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Olympiacos and Monaco

Touré joined [Olympiacos](/wiki/Olympiacos_F.C. "Olympiacos F.C.") in 2005. He was described as "the new [Patrick Vieira](/wiki/Patrick_Vieira "Patrick Vieira")" by his older brother. Olympiacos won the double that season, and Touré was one of their key players. His performances in Greece were impressive and attracted interest from many clubs.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Touré signed for French [Ligue 1](/wiki/Ligue_1 "Ligue 1") club [Monaco](/wiki/AS_Monaco_FC "AS Monaco FC") in August 2006.[[17]](#cite_note-17) However, he had a difficult relationship with the incumbent manager, [László Bölöni](/wiki/L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_B%C3%B6l%C3%B6ni "László Bölöni"), with Touré claiming Bölöni refused to play him in his preferred midfield position.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Bölöni was soon sacked with Monaco languishing in the second-last position in the league table.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

[Laurent Banide](/wiki/Laurent_Banide "Laurent Banide") replaced Bölöni and Touré subsequently became an influential player in the second half of the season, scoring five times to help haul the club out of relegation difficulties.[[20]](#cite_note-20) He had established himself as a key midfielder, and with interest from around Europe he decided to join [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") in the summer of 2007.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Barcelona

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Yaya_Tour%C3%A9.JPG/250px-Yaya_Tour%C3%A9.JPG)](/wiki/File:Yaya_Tour%C3%A9.JPG)

Touré with [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") in 2009

Touré joined Spanish [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") club Barcelona for €10 million (£6.7 million)[[22]](#cite_note-22) and made his official debut for the club on 26 August 2007 during the [2007–08 La Liga](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_La_Liga "2007–08 La Liga") season opener against [Racing de Santander](/wiki/Racing_de_Santander "Racing de Santander").[[23]](#cite_note-23) He scored his first goal in a La Liga match against [Athletic Bilbao](/wiki/Athletic_Bilbao "Athletic Bilbao") on 2 September 2007, which Barcelona won 3–1.[[24]](#cite_note-24) His first [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") match for Barcelona was against [Schalke 04](/wiki/FC_Schalke_04 "FC Schalke 04") in the [2007–08 quarter-final](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_UEFA_Champions_League_knockout_stage "2007–08 UEFA Champions League knockout stage"), securing a 2–0 aggregate victory and a berth in the semi-final.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

During the early stages of the [2008–09 season](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_FC_Barcelona_season "2008–09 FC Barcelona season"), newly appointed manager [Pep Guardiola](/wiki/Pep_Guardiola "Pep Guardiola") favoured [Sergio Busquets](/wiki/Sergio_Busquets "Sergio Busquets") in the defensive role of Barcelona's midfield trio. In the [2009 Champions League final](/wiki/2009_UEFA_Champions_League_final "2009 UEFA Champions League final"), Touré played at [centre-back](/wiki/Centre-back "Centre-back") due to injuries and suspensions of first-choice defenders, despite having only played there twice before for Barcelona.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In late June 2010, Barcelona confirmed that Touré would be allowed to leave the club in the summer window.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Manchester City

#### 2010–11: Debut season and FA Cup

On 2 July 2010, Touré signed a five-year contract with [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") club [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") for a fee of around £24 million.[[28]](#cite_note-28) He made his Premier League debut on 14 August in a 0–0 away draw against [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C.").[[29]](#cite_note-29) A week later, Touré impressed in the 3–0 home win over [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C."), forming a part of a three-man midfield with [Gareth Barry](/wiki/Gareth_Barry "Gareth Barry") and [Nigel de Jong](/wiki/Nigel_de_Jong "Nigel de Jong").[[30]](#cite_note-30) On 19 September, Touré scored his first City goal, against [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C.").[[31]](#cite_note-31) The goal was scored in the 70th minute and Touré was assisted by [Carlos Tevez](/wiki/Carlos_Tevez "Carlos Tevez"), who also scored in that match.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Touré had been seen to take up a more attacking position under [Roberto Mancini](/wiki/Roberto_Mancini "Roberto Mancini") and revelled in the position. In December, he scored a brilliant left-footed drive against [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.") and soon scored a second, which was later credited as an own goal because the ball rebounded off the post onto goalkeeper [Robert Green](/wiki/Robert_Green "Robert Green")'s back and bounced in.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In January 2011, Touré scored his third goal against [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") in a delightful counter-attack move, with Touré making up 90 yards to latch onto the ball and fire it past the goalkeeper, with Manchester City eventually winning 4–3.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Touré's fifth goal for City came on 25 February in a [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") match against [Aris](/wiki/Aris_Thessaloniki_F.C. "Aris Thessaloniki F.C."). The goal was a deflected strike to make the score 3–0 to Manchester City. On 16 April 2011, Touré scored the only goal of the game in a Man of the Match performance against [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") in the FA Cup semi-final.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Touré continued his [Wembley](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") success by scoring the only goal in his team's 1–0 win over [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C.") in the [2011 FA Cup final](/wiki/2011_FA_Cup_final "2011 FA Cup final"),[[36]](#cite_note-36) ending Manchester City's 35-year wait for a major trophy,[[37]](#cite_note-37) and writing himself into Manchester City folklore in the process.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

#### 2011–12: First Premier League title

The [2011–12 season](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Premier_League "2011–12 Premier League") began strongly as Manchester City led the table for the majority of the season, with Touré playing a vital part in midfield. On 21 December 2011, in a 3–0 home win over Stoke City, Touré set new Premier League records for most passes attempted (168) and most completed (157) in a single match.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) His efforts and performances were rewarded as he was crowned [African Footballer of the Year](/wiki/African_Footballer_of_the_Year "African Footballer of the Year") for 2011, an impressive achievement for a midfielder, since the previous 12 awards have gone to African forwards.[[7]](#cite_note-AFOTY-7)

As the 2011–12 came to a close, Manchester City lost ground to Manchester United in the Premier League title race. After a defeat by Arsenal on 8 April 2012,[[41]](#cite_note-41) an eight-point deficit had been established between City and leaders United, with most writing off City's chances of winning the league with six matches remaining. However, City defeated [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") 4–0, [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C.") 6–1, and Wolverhampton Wanderers 2–0 with United slipping-up, meaning the deficit at the top was now only three points with only three matches to play. A victory at home to Manchester United would move City to top on goal difference. The match was keenly anticipated as one of the most important matches the Premier League's 20-year history.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) Manchester City won the match, thanks to [Vincent Kompany](/wiki/Vincent_Kompany "Vincent Kompany") headed goal just before half-time. Touré's performance throughout the match garnered many plaudits in the media, describing his performance as "dominant" and "magnificent".[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Touré scored a brace on 6 May in City's 2–0 win over [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C."), moving three points above Manchester United with one match left to play in the Premier League season.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Touré played the full first half before coming off due to injury as City defeated [Queens Park Rangers](/wiki/Queens_Park_Rangers_F.C. "Queens Park Rangers F.C.") 3–2 on the final day of the season, assisting [Pablo Zabaleta](/wiki/Pablo_Zabaleta "Pablo Zabaleta")'s opening goal in the 39th minute, to secure the first league championship for City in 44 years.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

#### 2012–2014: Collective and individual success

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Yaya_Tour%C3%A9_2012-11-21_1.jpg/250px-Yaya_Tour%C3%A9_2012-11-21_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yaya_Tour%C3%A9_2012-11-21_1.jpg)

Touré playing for [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") in 2012

Touré started the [2012–13 season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_Premier_League "2012–13 Premier League") with a goal in the [FA Community Shield](/wiki/FA_Community_Shield "FA Community Shield") in the 3–2 win over [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") in [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.")'s stadium [Villa Park](/wiki/Villa_Park "Villa Park"). This goal was followed up by scoring first for City in two games, against Liverpool at [Anfield](/wiki/Anfield "Anfield") and against Queens Park Rangers at home. In City's opening [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_UEFA_Champions_League "2012–13 UEFA Champions League") match of the season, away to [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_CF "Real Madrid CF"), Touré produced a stellar individual performance, assisting an [Edin Džeko](/wiki/Edin_D%C5%BEeko "Edin Džeko") goal and continually worrying the hosts. However, despite his best efforts, City lost 3–2 to a late [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") goal. He also scored in the [Manchester derby](/wiki/Manchester_derby "Manchester derby"), making it 2–1 to Manchester United, but City eventually lost 3–2. On 20 December 2012, Touré was crowned the 2012 African Player of the Year award at a ceremony in [Accra](/wiki/Accra "Accra"), Ghana. It is the second time he has won the award.[[47]](#cite_note-47) On 24 February 2013, after returning from the [2013 Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/2013_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2013 Africa Cup of Nations"), Touré scored the opening goal as City beat Chelsea 2–0 in the Premier League.[[48]](#cite_note-48) On 4 April 2013, Touré signed a new contract with City, keeping him at the club until 2017.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

Touré scored his first goal of the [2013–14](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_Manchester_City_F.C._season "2013–14 Manchester City F.C. season") season with a free kick as City defeated Newcastle United 4–0 in their opening [Premier League](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_Premier_League "2013–14 Premier League") match. On 22 September 2013, he scored the second goal in a 4–1 derby win over Manchester United[[50]](#cite_note-50) and was named man of the match.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Under City manager [Manuel Pellegrini](/wiki/Manuel_Pellegrini "Manuel Pellegrini"), Touré was promoted to [vice-captain](/wiki/Vice-captain_(association_football) "Vice-captain (association football)"). He regularly [captained](/wiki/Captain_(association_football) "Captain (association football)") the team in the first half of the 2013–14 season in the absence of Vincent Kompany.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) On 2 December, Touré was named the 2013 [BBC African Footballer of the Year](/wiki/BBC_African_Footballer_of_the_Year "BBC African Footballer of the Year").[[54]](#cite_note-54)

On 4 December 2013, Touré scored twice for Manchester City in a 3–2 win at West Bromwich Albion. This took his tally of league goals to seven, his highest in a single Premier League season.[[55]](#cite_note-55) On 2 March 2014, Touré scored the equalising goal for Manchester City in the [2014 League Cup final](/wiki/2014_Football_League_Cup_final "2014 Football League Cup final") against [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.").[[56]](#cite_note-56)
The match ended in a 3–1 win for Manchester City.[[57]](#cite_note-57) He scored the first [hat-trick](/wiki/Hat-trick_(association_football) "Hat-trick (association football)") of his career on 22 March in a 5–0 home win over [Fulham](/wiki/Fulham_F.C. "Fulham F.C.").[[58]](#cite_note-58) Touré scored the third goal for Manchester City on 25 March in the Manchester Derby at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford"), helping City to a 3–0 victory over their local rivals.[[59]](#cite_note-59) On 18 April, Touré was named as one of the six players on the shortlist for the [PFA Players' Player of the Year](/wiki/PFA_Players%27_Player_of_the_Year "PFA Players' Player of the Year") award[[60]](#cite_note-60) before being named in the [PFA Team of the Year](/wiki/PFA_Team_of_the_Year "PFA Team of the Year").[[61]](#cite_note-61)

On 7 May 2014, Touré scored his 20th Premier League goal of the season in a 4–0 win against Aston Villa, becoming only the second midfielder to score 20 goals in a Premier League season, after [Frank Lampard](/wiki/Frank_Lampard "Frank Lampard"). His performances helped City to a second Premier League title in three years.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

On 20 May, Touré's agent posted on [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") that he had become unhappy at Manchester City due to the time it took City to give him a new deal in 2013 and was upset that no-one from the club wished him happy birthday. His agent, Dimitri Seluk, told [BBC Sport](/wiki/BBC_Sport "BBC Sport") that Toure was "very upset" and could leave City. Although he initially denied these rumours on Twitter, Touré later tweeted, "Everything Dimitri said is true. He speaks for me. I will explain after the [World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup")."[[63]](#cite_note-63)

Touré completed more passes than any other player throughout the 2013–14 Premier League season, with 1,169 successful passes in total; he also made an average of 76.41 passes per game, and achieved an average passing accuracy of 90.76% throughout the course of the entire season.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

#### 2014–2018: Later seasons and departure

On 5 November 2014, in a home [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_UEFA_Champions_League "2014–15 UEFA Champions League") group fixture against CSKA Moscow, Touré scored City's equaliser in the eighth minute after [Seydou Doumbia](/wiki/Seydou_Doumbia "Seydou Doumbia") had taken under two minutes to put CSKA ahead. City were already 2–1 down and had [Fernandinho](/wiki/Fernandinho_(footballer,_born_May_1985) "Fernandinho (footballer, born May 1985)") sent off when Touré was dismissed for shoving [Roman Eremenko](/wiki/Roman_Eremenko "Roman Eremenko"). The result put City into last place in their group.[[65]](#cite_note-65) On 1 March 2015, Touré started the Premier League match against Liverpool and his brother [Kolo Touré](/wiki/Kolo_Tour%C3%A9 "Kolo Touré") came on as an 83rd-minute substitute, marking the first time the two brothers faced each other in a competitive match.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

On 10 August 2015, Touré scored as Manchester City defeated West Bromwich Albion 3–0 at [The Hawthorns](/wiki/The_Hawthorns "The Hawthorns") in their opening fixture of the [2015–16 Premier League](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Premier_League "2015–16 Premier League") season.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Although he played in the second leg of the Champions League play-off against [Steaua București](/wiki/FC_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Steaua București"), Guardiola opted to omit Touré from City's Champions League squad.[[68]](#cite_note-68) Touré's agent, Dimitri Seluk, subsequently stated that Guardiola humiliated Touré by omitting him for the first team. In response, Guardiola said Touré would not be included in the team until he receives an apology.[[69]](#cite_note-69) A few weeks after a public apology to Guardiola on behalf of his agent, Touré made a surprising return to the first team on 16 November, starting against [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C.") in which he scored two goals to earn City a 2–1 away win.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

It was announced on 4 May 2018 that Touré would leave Manchester City at the end of the [2017–18 season](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Manchester_City_F.C._season "2017–18 Manchester City F.C. season").[[71]](#cite_note-71) His final appearance came in the 3–1 win over [Brighton & Hove Albion](/wiki/Brighton_%26_Hove_Albion_F.C. "Brighton & Hove Albion F.C.").[[72]](#cite_note-72)

### Later career

Toure re-signed for [Super League Greece](/wiki/Super_League_Greece "Super League Greece") club Olympiacos on 2 September 2018.[[73]](#cite_note-73) His contract with Olympiacos was terminated in December 2018 by mutual agreement.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

On 3 July 2019, Touré joined [China League One](/wiki/China_League_One "China League One") club [Qingdao Huanghai](/wiki/Qingdao_Huanghai_F.C. "Qingdao Huanghai F.C.").[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76) He left the club on 1 January 2020.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] He started to work with his advisor, in Asia, [Richard Harcus](/w/index.php?title=Richard_Harcus&action=edit&redlink=1 "Richard Harcus (page does not exist)") to help with his transition into coaching, with a lot of speculation as it whether he would continue playing in Asia for another season.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

## International career

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Yaya_Toure_9229.JPG/250px-Yaya_Toure_9229.JPG)](/wiki/File:Yaya_Toure_9229.JPG)

Touré playing for the [Ivory Coast](/wiki/Ivory_Coast_national_football_team "Ivory Coast national football team") in 2012

Touré was a regular member of the [Ivory Coast national team](/wiki/Ivory_Coast_national_football_team "Ivory Coast national football team") from his debut in 2004 to the end of his international career after 2015. In 2014, he was named captain of the team following the retirement of [Didier Drogba](/wiki/Didier_Drogba "Didier Drogba").[[78]](#cite_note-78) Touré won his 100th international [cap](/wiki/Cap_(association_football) "Cap (association football)") in his side's 0–0 draw with [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon_national_football_team "Cameroon national football team") on 19 November 2014.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

Touré appeared in all three of Ivory Coast's matches at the [2006 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the [2010](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup "2010 FIFA World Cup") tournament, he again played in all three of his nation's games, scoring the opening goal in a 3–0 win over [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea_national_football_team "North Korea national football team").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Touré made his third and final World Cup tournament appearance during the [2014 competition](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup"), and captained the team in their opening match, a 2–1 defeat of [Japan](/wiki/Japan_national_football_team "Japan national football team").[[80]](#cite_note-80)

Touré represented the Ivory Coast at six [Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/Africa_Cup_of_Nations "Africa Cup of Nations") tournaments, in [2006](/wiki/2006_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2006 Africa Cup of Nations"), [2008](/wiki/2008_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2008 Africa Cup of Nations"), [2010](/wiki/2010_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2010 Africa Cup of Nations"), [2012](/wiki/2012_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2012 Africa Cup of Nations"), [2013](/wiki/2013_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2013 Africa Cup of Nations") and [2015](/wiki/2015_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2015 Africa Cup of Nations"); his side ended as runners-up in the 2006 and 2012 editions, and won the tournament in 2015. Touré was named in the [CAF](/wiki/Confederation_of_African_Football "Confederation of African Football")'s team of the tournament in [2008](/wiki/2008_Africa_Cup_of_Nations#Best_XI "2008 Africa Cup of Nations"), [2012](/wiki/2012_Africa_Cup_of_Nations#Team_of_the_tournament "2012 Africa Cup of Nations") and [2015](/wiki/2015_Africa_Cup_of_Nations#Awards "2015 Africa Cup of Nations").[[81]](#cite_note-81) On 4 February 2015, Touré scored the opening goal in the Ivory Coast's 3–1 semi-final victory over [DR Congo](/wiki/DR_Congo_national_football_team "DR Congo national football team") to qualify the team for their third Africa Cup of Nations final in nine years.[[82]](#cite_note-82) On 8 February, in the [final](/wiki/2015_Africa_Cup_of_Nations_final "2015 Africa Cup of Nations final") held at the [Estadio de Bata](/wiki/Estadio_de_Bata "Estadio de Bata") in Equatorial Guinea, Touré captained the Ivory Coast as they won a first AFCON title since [1992](/wiki/1992_African_Cup_of_Nations "1992 African Cup of Nations"), defeating [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana_national_football_team "Ghana national football team") in a penalty shoot-out.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

On 20 September 2016, Touré announced his retirement from international football.[[84]](#cite_note-84) He returned to the national team set-up in March 2018,[[85]](#cite_note-85) but would play no further games for the nation before his eventual retirement from professional football.

## Style of play

Formerly a [striker](/wiki/Striker_(association_football) "Striker (association football)"),[[86]](#cite_note-4-4-2-86) Touré played primarily in the [centre](/wiki/Midfielder#Central_midfielder "Midfielder") of the pitch as either a [holding midfielder](/wiki/Holding_midfielder "Holding midfielder") or as a [box-to-box midfielder](/wiki/Box-to-box_midfielder "Box-to-box midfielder"), often switching between offensive and defensive stances throughout matches in the latter role. Touré was a complete and versatile midfielder.[[5]](#cite_note-centreback-5)[[87]](#cite_note-end-87) He was also capable of playing as a [centre-back](/wiki/Centre-back "Centre-back")[[5]](#cite_note-centreback-5) or in the [number 10](/wiki/Squad_number_(association_football) "Squad number (association football)") role.[[87]](#cite_note-end-87) His key strengths were his passing range, vision and physical ability. He was gifted with good technique and ball control, although he also excelled defensively at breaking down opposing attacking plays and winning back possession through his powerful tackling ability.[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[86]](#cite_note-4-4-2-86) He was known for his pace, stamina and physical presence, which was often combined with runs from midfield, and he had been referred to as a "human train" and a "colossus" by various pundits.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

Touré was frequently pushed forward during the latter part of matches, including in the penultimate match of the 2011–12 season against Newcastle United where Touré scored two late goals to win the match, and put Manchester City at the top of the league.[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91) He was also capable of scoring goals due to his powerful striking ability from distance,[[92]](#cite_note-92) and his heading ability, as well as his adeptness at scoring from [free kicks](/wiki/Free_kick_(association_football) "Free kick (association football)") and [penalties](/wiki/Penalty_kick_(association_football) "Penalty kick (association football)"). [Jonathan Wilson](/wiki/Jonathan_Wilson_(writer) "Jonathan Wilson (writer)"), when writing for *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* in 2013, described Touré as a holding midfielder who can "make tackles," and who was "capable of regaining the ball," while also noting that he frequently functioned "as the more creative player alongside a destroyer." He also added that "he likes to make forward surges." As such, he labelled Touré as a "carrier" or "surger," namely "a player capable of making late runs or carrying the ball at his feet."[[93]](#cite_note-WilsonHolding13-93)

Touré had in his repertoire an unusual method of striking the ball, referred to by Norwegian footballer [Alexander Tettey](/wiki/Alexander_Tettey "Alexander Tettey") as a "Yaya", and described by [BBC Sport](/wiki/BBC_Sport "BBC Sport")'s [Pat Nevin](/wiki/Pat_Nevin "Pat Nevin") as a way of striking the ball with a far back area of the foot close to the heel, allowing the ball to move in a straight line which a goalkeeper can find hard to read.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

## Coaching career

Embarking on his coaching pathway Yaya Touré sat his UEFA licence through the [Welsh FA (FAW)](/wiki/Football_Association_of_Wales "Football Association of Wales") with the guidance of his then Asian agent [Richard Harcus.](/w/index.php?title=Richard_Harcus.&action=edit&redlink=1 "Richard Harcus. (page does not exist)") During [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") he assisted at [Leyton Orient FC](/wiki/Leyton_Orient_F.C. "Leyton Orient F.C.").[[95]](#cite_note-95)

On 10 February 2021, Touré joined [Ukrainian Premier League](/wiki/Ukrainian_Premier_League "Ukrainian Premier League") club [Olimpik Donetsk](/wiki/FC_Olimpik_Donetsk "FC Olimpik Donetsk") as assistant manager.[[96]](#cite_note-96) He left the position after 4 months at the club.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

On 14 June 2021, Touré was appointed as an assistant coach by [Russian Premier League](/wiki/Russian_Premier_League "Russian Premier League") club [Akhmat Grozny](/wiki/FC_Akhmat_Grozny "FC Akhmat Grozny") until June 2022.[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99) Touré left the club on 13 December 2021.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

After a spell coaching part-time, Touré became an under-16 coach at [Tottenham Hotspur's academy](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C._Under-23s_and_Academy "Tottenham Hotspur F.C. Under-23s and Academy") in August 2022.[[101]](#cite_note-101) The following June, he moved to Belgian club [Standard Liège](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge "Standard Liège") as assistant manager.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

On 3 November 2023, Touré was named as assistant coach of the [Saudi Arabia national team](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia_national_football_team "Saudi Arabia national football team"), to join the staff of his former manager at Manchester City [Roberto Mancini](/wiki/Roberto_Mancini "Roberto Mancini").[[103]](#cite_note-103)

## Personal life

Touré is the younger brother of [Kolo Touré](/wiki/Kolo_Tour%C3%A9 "Kolo Touré"), who was his teammate at Manchester City and the Ivory Coast national team. Their younger brother [Ibrahim Touré](/wiki/Ibrahim_Tour%C3%A9_(footballer,_born_1985) "Ibrahim Touré (footballer, born 1985)") was also a footballer, who died on 19 June 2014 at the age of 28 after a short battle with cancer.[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105) Touré is Muslim.[[106]](#cite_note-deniesconsuming-106)

In October 2013, Touré joined a campaign against elephant poaching, becoming a goodwill ambassador for the [United Nations Environment Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Environment_Programme "United Nations Environment Programme").[[107]](#cite_note-bbc131029-107)

On 13 December 2016, Touré pleaded guilty to drink driving. He was arrested by police on 28 November after being pulled over and found to be more than twice over the legal driving limit.[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-acceptscharge-109) Touré said he had not "intentionally consumed alcohol", explaining to the court he had consumed diet cola from a jug at a party without realising there was brandy in it.[[109]](#cite_note-acceptscharge-109) He conceded that his drink tasted "odd" and that he felt tired after consuming it, but did not suspect he was drunk.[[106]](#cite_note-deniesconsuming-106) Judge Gary Lucy said it was "inconceivable" that he was unaware that he was drinking alcohol.[[106]](#cite_note-deniesconsuming-106) Touré was banned from driving for 18 months, and was fined £54,000 – at the time the largest fine for drink driving in British legal history.[[106]](#cite_note-deniesconsuming-106)

In 2018, Yaya Touré acquired British citizenship.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

## Awards and nominations

Touré was nominated in the Personality of the Year category at the [2014 MTV Africa Music Awards](/wiki/MTV_Africa_Music_Awards_2014 "MTV Africa Music Awards 2014").[[111]](#cite_note-111) In July 2014, Touré was nominated for Prize in Entertainment at the 2014 [The Future Africa Awards](/wiki/The_Future_Awards_Africa "The Future Awards Africa").[[112]](#cite_note-112) In December 2015, Touré won the BBC African Footballer of the Year on a public vote for the second time, after leading the Ivory Coast to victory in the 2015 African Cup of Nations.[[113]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-113)
